text: Good. Greek, "these things, and he who possess the knowledge of the law will find her," (Calmet) or, "he who keeps the law, will receive her." (Grabe) (1 Corinthians 4:20.) (Haydock)<hr>All who resolve to be virtuous, will have God's grace, which preventeth them, and continueth to afford them assistance. (Worthington)
Married. Literally, "from virginity." (Haydock) Those who have been espoused in youth have the most durable love for one another, Proverbs 2:17., and Malachias 2:14. (Calmet)

She. Or Greek, "he shall open his mouth, (6.) he shall find joy and a crown of exultation, and shall inherit," etc. (Haydock)<hr>Church, or assembly of the people. In both, the wise shall be heard with respect. (Calmet)

Praise. Or Greek, "a proverb," Proverbs 17:7., and 26:7. (Calmet)<hr>David invites even dragons to praise God, but sinners never. (St. Chrysostom, orat. prebyt.) (Psalm 49:16.)<hr>The person who preaches well, and lives not accordingly, injures God. (Worthington)
Wisdom. Greek, "it was not sent to him, (Calmet) or he was not commissioned by the Lord. For praise shall be spoken in wisdom, and the Lord will give him success."

She. Ignorance and vice are defects, and consequently not the work of God, but of the perversion of free-will. See James 1:13., and Ezechiel 33:11. (St. Augustine, Grat. et. lib. ii.) (Calmet)<hr>Beza says, that God ordained Adam's fall, but for a good end; and decreed justly what men unjustly perform, (ref. Castel. ii.) which is refuted here, and Deuteronomy 7:11., etc. (Worthington)

Counsel. Many explain this of Adam, before his fall, when he stood not in need of asking continually for God's assistance. (St. Augustine, correp. 2:n. 35.)<hr>But if we examine the context, it shews that man, in his present state, is declared inexcusable if he yield to sin, as he has still free-will, which may avoid it, with the grace of God, (Calmet) which is always ready to support us. Greek subjoins, (ver. 16.) "if thou wilt, thou shall keep the commandments, even to execute faithfully the good pleasure. (17.) He." etc. (Haydock)
Precepts. These would never be imposed, if man were not free. (Calmet)

And fire. Or, good and evil, as the Vulgate explains it, (ver. 18.) though the Greek be silent. (Haydock)<hr>The law written on the heart, as well as on the tables of stone, shews man his duty;; and he has free-will to comply with it, Deuteronomy 30:15., and Jeremias 21:8. (Calmet)<hr>He may enjoy every advantage of life, denoted by these two elements. (Lyr.[Lyranus?]) An quod in his vitae causa est, haec perdidit exul His nova fit conjux; haec duo magna putant. (Ovid, Fast. 4.)
Commanded. He only permitted the wickedness of Semei, (2 Kings 16:10.) and the persecutors of his people, whose hearts are already bent on evil. (St. Augustine, Grat. et lib. xx.)
